title: Microscopic Nonaffine Deformation Theory of LAOS in Polymers
abstract: We develop a molecularly motivated framework connecting large-amplitude oscillatory shear (LAOS) nonlinearities in entangled polymers to frequency-dependent nonaffine relaxation in disordered solids. The central idea is that the first harmonic in LAOS measures the residual phase-locked elastic response, whereas the higher harmonics encode the Fourier signature of strain-dependent nonaffine relaxation. The finite-amplitude modulus is interpreted as a local tangent stiffness of the evolving microstructure, in the spirit of elastoplastic and incremental nonaffine models. For entangled polymers, the analogue of the decreasing coordination number in cage-breaking theories of glass mechanics is identified not with the tube-orientation tensor itself, but with the fraction of surviving tube constraints. This distinction leads naturally to a crossover description controlled by a characteristic strain amplitude $γ_c$, rather than by universal fixed power-law exponents. The fitted value $N_{\max}\simeq1.72$ indicates that the present experimental data approach a strong but not fully saturated nonlinear state, remaining below the ideal limiting value predicted for complete constraint collapse. Finally, a constraint-counting argument combining an eight-chain affine network representation with the central-force nonaffine isostatic threshold gives a limiting estimate $|\mathrm{NLI}|_{\max}=3$. The results support the interpretation of the NLI as a Fourier-resolved dynamic nonaffinity parameter and establish a bridge between tube-based polymer dynamics, LAOS harmonic analysis, elastoplastic rheology, and microscopic nonaffine lattice dynamics.
summary: This paper provides a microscopic molecular basis for the nonlinear viscoelasticity observed in polymers during large-amplitude oscillations. It reinterprets the Nonlinearity Index as a measure of how structural constraints break down, linking polymer physics to the broader theory of nonaffine deformations in glasses.
main_problem: Bridging the gap between tube-based polymer dynamics and the microscopic nonaffine theory of disordered elasticity to explain the origin of nonlinearities in Large-Amplitude Oscillatory Shear (LAOS).
main_result: The authors establish that the Nonlinearity Index (NLI) is a dynamic nonaffinity parameter measuring the transfer of elastic response from coherent tube elasticity to nonlinear relaxation channels, predicting a theoretical upper bound of |NLI|max = 3.
method: The study uses a multiscale framework combining Fourier-transform rheology, nonaffine lattice dynamics, and a constraint-counting argument based on the eight-chain network model.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of entangled polymer melts and concentrated solutions, modeled using a combination of Doi-Edwards tube theory and nonaffine deformation theory for amorphous solids.
key_observables: Nonlinearity Index (NLI), first-harmonic storage modulus (G'1), nonlinear modulus (G'NL), and the tube survival fraction (q).
important_parameters: Characteristic strain amplitude (gamma_c), maximum nonlinearity (Nmax), crossover exponent (m), and the nonaffine isostatic threshold.
main_assumptions: The framework assumes a mapping between tube constraint loss and cage-breaking, and uses an eight-chain representation to estimate mechanical coordination.
figures_summary: The paper includes experimental master curves for the NLI, scaling plots of the modulus components, and a summary table of effective scaling regimes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of connecting tube theory to nonaffine elasticity, develops a mathematical framework for LAOS harmonics, proposes a crossover scaling law, performs experimental fitting, and concludes with a theoretical derivation of the NLI upper bound.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Experimental master curve of the normalized nonlinear elastic contribution, NLI = −G′ NL/G′ 1, plotted as a function of the normalized strain amplitude γ0/γLR 0 . Symbols are experimental data from the literature [11], while the solid line is the theoretical crossover fit given by Eq. (83). The data include polymers with markedly different molecular architectures, including predominantly linear linear-low-density polyethylene (LLDPE), highly branched low-density polyethylene (LDPE), randomly branched emulsion-polymerized styrene–butadiene rubber (e- SBR), and solution-polymerized partially coupled styrene–butadiene rubber (s-SBR).
